papers.title: DARTCONTROL: A DIFFUSION-BASED AUTOREGRES-SIVE MOTION MODEL FOR REAL-TIME TEXT-DRIVEN MOTION CONTROL

strengths: The paper proposes an autoregressive latent motion primitive representation that strikes a practical balance between temporal coherence and controllability, distinct from prior sequence-level diffusion or frame-level AR models.

Integrates two complementary control mechanisms over the same latent space: gradient-based latent noise optimization and an RL policy that outputs latent diffusion noises as actions, enabling diverse downstream tasks without paired control signals.
Uses a VAE on motion primitives to denoise dataset artifacts and simplify the diffusion target distribution; the observation that this reduces jitter is compelling and well-motivated.

Comprehensive evaluation on text-conditioned temporal composition against strong baselines (FlowMDM, TEACH, DoubleTake, T2M-GPT*), with dataset-level metrics, transition smoothness (jerk), and human preference studies.
Demonstrates strong performance on text-conditioned in-betweening with quantitative comparisons to DNO and OmniControl, including goal error and skating.
RL control is validated against GAMMA with multiple locomotion styles, reporting success rate, skating, and floor distance at >200 FPS.
Clear problem formulation, model diagrams, and concise autoregressive rollout algorithm; the separation of VAE (frozen) and denoiser training is easy to follow.
Method details like scheduled sampling, classifier-free guidance, and canonicalization are explained with sufficient intuition.
Addresses a critical gap: online, real-time, text-driven motion with precise spatial control, where most diffusion approaches are offline and slow.
The latent control view (no paired control data needed) is versatile and can simplify building task-specific controllers in animation/VR/robotics pipelines.
weaknesses: Dependence on frame-aligned action annotations (BABEL) is strong; performance degrades with coarse sentence-level labels (HML3D), limiting broader applicability.
Over-parameterized frame representation mixing rotations and joint positions may risk internal inconsistency; the paper relies on VAE regularization but lacks explicit analysis of consistency errors.
Long-horizon stability and drift are not systematically quantified beyond transition jerk; error accumulation across very long sequences is a practical concern for AR models.
Human-scene interaction results are qualitative; no quantitative collision/contact metrics are reported for these experiments despite available SDF machinery.

RL comparisons are limited (GAMMA only, walking); no head-to-head with recent diffusion-policy character controls or ASE-style skills; generalization to dynamic obstacles or OOD goals is not evaluated.
Latent noise optimization cost is not profiled; scalability to complex scenes/long horizons could be computationally heavy due to backpropagation through diffusion and the decoder.
Some crucial training details (e.g., exact loss weights, history/primitive ablations) are deferred to appendices; the main paper would benefit from summarizing key ablation takeaways.
The canonicalization steps during rollout and their effect on global trajectory drift could be spelled out more explicitly.

While related prior art is broadly covered, empirical comparisons to other real-time/AR diffusion character control methods (e.g., Shi et al. 2024, Chen et al. 2024) are absent; even if text is unsupported there, speed/quality comparisons could be informative.
Quantitative evaluation on additional temporal composition baselines that are online would strengthen claims about real-time superiority and semantic alignment.
